harmful_qa.name: HarmfulQA Annotation Taxonomy
harmful_qa._source_paper: Bhardwaj & Poria, Red-Teaming Large Language Models using Chain of Utterances for Safety-Alignment (arXiv:2308.09662)
harmful_qa._provenance: 10 academic 'topic' names verbatim from Table 7 of arXiv:2308.09662 / declare-lab HarmfulQA HF card. CRITICAL CAVEAT: HarmfulQA is NOT a harm-type taxonomy — it is a topic taxonomy of academic disciplines used to seed harmful question generation via the Chain-of-Utterances jailbreak. Each topic spans ~10 subtopics × ~20 harmful questions; the 'harm' is uniform across topics (the question itself is harmful), so these categories function as DOMAINS the harmful question is framed in, not categories of harm.

policies.policy_description: Topic domain seeding harmful questions framed in scientific or technical disciplines.
block_activities: Harmful questions framed via Physics, Chemistry, Biology, Astronomy, Geology, Computer Science, Engineering, Environmental Science, Neuroscience, or Robotics
policies._provenance: Topic name verbatim from Table 7 of arXiv:2308.09662; subtopic list verbatim.

policies.policy_description: Topic domain seeding harmful questions framed via historical or cultural disciplines.
block_activities: Harmful questions framed via Ancient/Medieval/Modern/World/Art History, Cultural Anthropology, Archaeology, Historical Figures/Events, Social Movements
policies._provenance: Topic name and subtopic list verbatim from Table 7 of arXiv:2308.09662.

policies.policy_description: Topic domain seeding harmful questions framed via mathematical or logical disciplines.
block_activities: Harmful questions framed via Algebra, Geometry, Calculus, Statistics, Number Theory, Logic and Reasoning, Mathematical Modeling, Probability Theory, Cryptography, Game Theory
policies._provenance: Topic and subtopics verbatim from Table 7 of arXiv:2308.09662.

policies.policy_description: Topic domain seeding harmful questions framed via literary or linguistic disciplines.
block_activities: Harmful questions framed via Fiction, Poetry, Drama, Literary Analysis/Genres/Theory, Linguistics, Language Acquisition, Comparative Literature, Translation Studies
policies._provenance: Topic and subtopics verbatim from Table 7 of arXiv:2308.09662.

policies.policy_description: Topic domain seeding harmful questions framed via philosophical or ethical disciplines.
block_activities: Harmful questions framed via Epistemology, Metaphysics, Ethics, Philosophy of Mind, Political Philosophy, Existentialism, Eastern Philosophy, Ethical Dilemmas, Moral Philosophy, Aesthetics
policies._provenance: Topic and subtopics verbatim from Table 7 of arXiv:2308.09662.

policies.policy_description: Topic domain seeding harmful questions framed via social-science disciplines.
block_activities: Harmful questions framed via Sociology, Psychology, Anthropology, Economics, Political Science, Gender Studies, Cultural Studies, Social Psychology, Urban Studies, Linguistic Anthropology
policies._provenance: Topic and subtopics verbatim from Table 7 of arXiv:2308.09662.

policies.policy_description: Topic domain seeding harmful questions framed via medical or health disciplines.
block_activities: Harmful questions framed via Anatomy, Physiology, Nutrition, Pharmacology, Medical Ethics, Disease Prevention, Healthcare Systems, Public Health, Alternative Medicine, Medical Research
policies._provenance: Topic and subtopics verbatim from Table 7 of arXiv:2308.09662.

policies.policy_description: Topic domain seeding harmful questions framed via geographical or environmental disciplines.
block_activities: Harmful questions framed via Physical/Human Geography, Geopolitics, Cartography, Environmental Conservation, Climate Change, Natural Disasters, Sustainable Development, Urban Planning, Ecological Systems
policies._provenance: Topic and subtopics verbatim from Table 7 of arXiv:2308.09662.

policies.policy_description: Topic domain seeding harmful questions framed via education-related disciplines.
block_activities: Harmful questions framed via Learning Theories, Curriculum Development, Educational Psychology, Instructional Design, Assessment and Evaluation, Special Education, Educational Technology, Classroom Management, Lifelong Learning, Educational Policy
policies._provenance: Topic and subtopics verbatim from Table 7 of arXiv:2308.09662.

policies.policy_description: Topic domain seeding harmful questions framed via business and economics disciplines.
block_activities: Harmful questions framed via Entrepreneurship, Marketing, Finance, Accounting, Business Strategy, Supply Chain Management, Economic Theory, International Trade, Consumer Behavior, Corporate Social Responsibility
policies._provenance: Topic and subtopics verbatim from Table 7 of arXiv:2308.09662.

harmful_qa._dataset_size_note: 1,960 harmful questions = 98 subtopics × 20 questions. Two subtopics (Chemistry under Science and Technology; Political Philosophy under Philosophy and Ethics) failed to retrieve enough harmful questions and were excluded.
harmful_qa._methodology_note: Questions are paired with multi-turn chain-of-utterance jailbreak conversations (CoU red-teaming) — the full record is a multi-turn dialogue, not a standalone Q.

do_not_answer.name: Do-Not-Answer Annotation Taxonomy
do_not_answer._source_paper: Wang et al., Do-Not-Answer: A Dataset for Evaluating Safeguards in LLMs (Findings of EACL 2024, arXiv:2308.13387)
do_not_answer._provenance: Risk-area names and 12 type-of-harm names quoted verbatim from the ar5iv HTML mirror of arXiv:2308.13387. Specific-harms level (60 / 61 leaves) summarized; full leaf list lives in the dataset's `specific_harms` column rather than the paper text.

policies.policy_description: Risk area covering content that compromises private or sensitive information by leaking or inferring it.
block_activities: Risks from leaking or inferring sensitive information about an organization or government
Compromise of privacy by leaking or inferring private information about a person or individual
policies._provenance: Risk-area name and the two harm-types verbatim from arXiv:2308.13387 taxonomy.

policies.policy_description: Risk area covering content that helps users perform illegal, unsafe, or unethical acts, or amplifies disinformation.
block_activities: Assisting illegal activities
Nudging or advising users to perform unethical or unsafe actions
Reducing the cost of disinformation campaigns
policies._provenance: Risk-area and harm-type names verbatim from arXiv:2308.13387.

policies.policy_description: Risk area covering content that demeans, excludes, or targets groups via stereotypes, hate speech, or sexual material.
block_activities: Social stereotypes and unfair discrimination
Toxic language (hate speech)
Adult content
policies._provenance: Risk-area and harm-type names verbatim from arXiv:2308.13387.

policies.policy_description: Risk area covering content that disseminates false, misleading, or inaccurate information, especially when consequential.
block_activities: Disseminating false or misleading information
Causing material harm by disseminating misinformation (e.g., medicine or law)
policies._provenance: Risk-area and harm-type names verbatim from arXiv:2308.13387.

policies.policy_description: Risk area covering harms that emerge from how users relate to the chatbot, including emotional dependence and anthropomorphization.
block_activities: Mental health or overreliance crisis
Treating chatbot as a human
policies._provenance: Risk-area and harm-type names verbatim from arXiv:2308.13387.

do_not_answer._taxonomy_note: The dataset card describes a 5-risk-area / 12-harm-type / ~60 specific-harm three-level hierarchy; the second level (12 types of harm) is enumerated above as block_activities under each risk area; the leaf level (specific_harms column on HF) is not enumerated in the paper text.

harmbench_behaviors.name: HarmBench Annotation Taxonomy

Prose category descriptions paraphrased from Section B.4 / Appendix of arXiv:2402.04249.

policies.policy_description: Behaviors covering harmful propaganda, election interference, defamation, and other misleading content (HarmBench SemanticCategory "misinformation_disinformation").
block_activities: Harmful propaganda generation

Generation of false news and misleading narratives
policies._provenance: Verbatim category column 'misinformation_disinformation'; activities paraphrased from Section B.4 of arXiv:2402.04249.

policies.policy_description: Behaviors covering hate speech, harassment, and encouragement of self-harm directed at people (HarmBench SemanticCategory "harassment_bullying").
block_activities: Hate speech against protected groups
Harassment and bullying of individuals
Encouraging or instructing self-harm
policies._provenance: Verbatim category column 'harassment_bullying'; scope quoted from Section B.4 ("hate speech and encouraging self-harm") of arXiv:2402.04249.

policies.policy_description: Behaviors covering fraud, trafficking, illicit weapons acquisition, theft, and violent crime (HarmBench SemanticCategory "illegal").
block_activities: Fraud and scams

policies.policy_description: Behaviors that fall outside the six narrow categories: graphic content, unsafe practices, and privacy violations (HarmBench SemanticCategory "harmful").
block_activities: Graphic violent or disturbing content

Privacy violations including doxxing
policies._provenance: Verbatim category column 'harmful'; description quoted from Section B.4 ('graphic content, unsafe practices, privacy violations') of arXiv:2402.04249.

harmbench_behaviors._functional_categories_note: HarmBench also defines FunctionalCategory ∈ {standard, contextual, copyright} per behavior (CSV-confirmed): 'standard' = self-contained behavior strings; 'contextual' = behavior strings paired with a context string; 'copyright' = requests for copyrighted text. The paper's Section 4.1 also references multimodal behaviors (image+text) as a fourth functional grouping in the multimodal track, but the public text-only CSV uses only the three functional values listed above.

anthropic_hh_rlhf.name: Anthropic HH-RLHF Annotation Taxonomy
anthropic_hh_rlhf._source_paper: Bai et al., Training a Helpful and Harmless Assistant with Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (arXiv:2204.05862); Ganguli et al., Red Teaming Language Models to Reduce Harms (arXiv:2209.07858)
anthropic_hh_rlhf._source_dataset: Anthropic/hh-rlhf (subdirectories: harmless-base, helpful-base, helpful-online, helpful-rejection-sampled, red-team-attempts)
anthropic_hh_rlhf._provenance: harmless-base preference annotations are pairwise (chosen vs rejected) with NO per-record harm taxonomy; the only published harm-type breakdown comes from Ganguli et al. 2209.07858 §3.5, which describes 'up to 2 of 20 total topic tags' assigned to a 1k-attack subsample of red-team-attempts. Ganguli et al. enumerates the top-5 most common tags verbatim and a partial list of less common tags; the paper does NOT publish a full table of all 20 tags. Categories below are therefore restricted to tag names that appear verbatim in arXiv:2209.07858 (Figure 9 and Section 3.5 prose).

policies.policy_description: Top-1 most frequent red-team harm tag in Ganguli et al. (2022): content reflecting unfair, biased, or discriminatory treatment.
block_activities: Stereotyping or denigrating people based on protected characteristics
Endorsing unequal treatment or systemic injustice
policies._provenance: Tag name 'Discrimination & injustice' verbatim from Figure 9 of arXiv:2209.07858; behaviors paraphrased.

policies.policy_description: Red-team harm tag covering slurs, hateful language, and offensive content directed at people or groups.
block_activities: Slurs and hateful epithets
Offensive or vulgar attacks on people or groups
policies._provenance: Tag name 'Hate speech & offensive language' verbatim from Figure 9 of arXiv:2209.07858.

policies.policy_description: Red-team harm tag covering content that depicts, threatens, or incites physical violence.
block_activities: Threats of violence
Incitement to attack people, groups, or institutions
Glorification of violent acts
policies._provenance: Tag name 'Violence & incitement' verbatim from Figure 9 of arXiv:2209.07858.

policies.policy_name: Non-violent unethical behavior (e.g., lying, cheating, etc.)
policies.policy_description: Red-team harm tag covering ethically wrong conduct that does not involve physical violence.
block_activities: Lying and deception
Cheating, fraud, and dishonest gain
Manipulation and betrayal of trust
policies._provenance: Tag name verbatim (with parenthetical) from Figure 9 / §3.5 of arXiv:2209.07858.

policies.policy_description: Red-team harm tag for repeated targeted abuse, intimidation, or harassment of an individual.
block_activities: Personal attacks and intimidation
Harassment campaigns or stalking
Coordinated mockery or shaming of a specific person
policies._provenance: Tag name 'Bullying & harassment' verbatim from arXiv:2209.07858 Figure 9.

policies.policy_description: Red-team harm tag covering physical, emotional, or sexual abuse, neglect, or exploitation of children.
block_activities: Child sexual abuse material or grooming
Physical or emotional abuse of children
Encouraging neglect or exploitation of minors
policies._provenance: Tag name 'Child Abuse' verbatim from §3.5 of arXiv:2209.07858 ('less common tags' list).

policies.policy_description: Red-team harm tag covering content that promotes, glorifies, or instructs self-injury or suicide.
block_activities: Methods or instructions for self-injury or suicide
Encouragement, glorification, or romanticization of self-harm
policies._provenance: Tag name 'Self harm' verbatim from §3.5 of arXiv:2209.07858.

policies.policy_description: Red-team harm tag covering coerced sexual activity, sexual exploitation, and human trafficking.
block_activities: Sex trafficking and forced sexual labor
Sexual coercion or exploitation of vulnerable people
Procurement of victims for sexual exploitation
policies._provenance: Tag name 'Sexual Exploitation & Human Trafficking' verbatim from §3.5 of arXiv:2209.07858.

policies.policy_description: Red-team harm tag covering content endorsing or facilitating terrorism or organized criminal activity.
block_activities: Endorsing terrorist groups or attacks
Operational support for organized crime
policies._provenance: Tag name 'Terrorism & organized crime' verbatim from §3.5 of arXiv:2209.07858.

policies.policy_description: Red-team harm tag covering cruelty, neglect, or abuse directed at animals.
block_activities: Cruelty toward or torture of animals
Neglect or exploitation of animals
policies._provenance: Tag name 'Animal abuse' verbatim from §3.5 of arXiv:2209.07858.

policies.policy_description: Catch-all red-team harm tag in Ganguli et al. 2022 for transcripts that do not fit a more specific category.
block_activities: Harmful content that does not match any other listed tag
policies._provenance: Tag 'Other' explicitly listed in §3.5 of arXiv:2209.07858.

_caveats: Ganguli et al. annotated ~1,000 of 38,961 red-team-attempts conversations with up to 2 of 20 total topic tags (§3.5). Only 11 tags appear verbatim in the paper's text/Figure 9 — the remaining ~9 tags are not enumerated in the published paper.
harmless-base subset has NO upstream harm taxonomy; preferences are pairwise-only.
Per the HF dataset card: data should NOT be used to SFT dialogue agents — it is reward-model preference data.

hatexplain.name: HateXplain Annotation Taxonomy
hatexplain._source_paper: Mathew et al., HateXplain: A Benchmark Dataset for Explainable Hate Speech Detection (AAAI 2021, arXiv:2012.10289)
hatexplain._provenance: Three-class label ('hatespeech', 'offensive', 'normal') and target-community list (Table 3 of arXiv:2012.10289) transcribed verbatim. Authors cite Davidson et al. 2017 for the label distinction; the paper does not provide formal per-label definitions, so descriptions paraphrase the paper's prose.

policies.policy_description: A post is labeled hatespeech when it expresses hate against a target community; the paper distinguishes hate from merely offensive language in line with Davidson et al. (2017).
block_activities: Attacking, dehumanizing, or calling for violence against a target community
Slurs and dehumanizing language directed at the target community
policies._provenance: Label name 'hatespeech' verbatim; description paraphrases Section 1 / Section 3 prose of arXiv:2012.10289.

policies.policy_description: A post is labeled offensive when it uses crude or insulting language without rising to hate against a protected community; the authors cite the Davidson et al. example of in-group reclamation (e.g., the n-word in African-American everyday speech and rap lyrics) as offensive but not hate speech.
block_activities: Crude insults, profanity, or slurs used outside a hateful targeting context
Aggressive or vulgar language not aimed at a protected community
policies._provenance: Label 'offensive' verbatim from paper / dataset; rationale paraphrased from arXiv:2012.10289 Section 1.

policies.policy_description: A post is labeled normal when it is neither hate speech nor offensive — i.e., does not target a protected community and does not use abusive language.
safe_activities: Neutral commentary, opinion, or news
Posts that mention a community without attacking or insulting it
policies._provenance: Label 'normal' verbatim; description paraphrased from arXiv:2012.10289.

tweet_eval_hate.name: TweetEval (hate) / SemEval-2019 Task 5 (HatEval) Annotation Taxonomy
tweet_eval_hate._source_paper: Barbieri et al., TweetEval: Unified Benchmark and Comparative Evaluation for Tweet Classification (Findings of EMNLP 2020, arXiv:2010.12421); the 'hate' split sources from Basile et al., SemEval-2019 Task 5: Multilingual Detection of Hate Speech Against Immigrants and Women in Twitter (ACL S19-2007).
tweet_eval_hate._provenance: Binary label (0=non-hate, 1=hate) and scope ('hate against immigrants and women') verbatim from cardiffnlp/tweet_eval HF card and Basile et al. S19-2007. SemEval-2019 Task 5 also defines two finer dimensions in Subtask B (TR target individual/group; AG aggressive); these are present in the original HatEval release but flattened to a single binary label in TweetEval.

policies.policy_description: Hate speech directed at immigrants, as defined by SemEval-2019 Task 5: "detection of hate speech against immigrants and women in Spanish and English messages extracted from Twitter."
block_activities: Dehumanizing language about immigrants
Calls for violence, expulsion, or exclusion of immigrants
Slurs and stereotypes targeting immigrant identity
policies._provenance: Target group 'immigrants' verbatim from SemEval-2019 Task 5 task definition (S19-2007); behavior examples paraphrased.

policies.policy_description: Hate speech directed at women, the second of the two protected targets defined by SemEval-2019 Task 5.
block_activities: Misogynistic slurs and dehumanizing language
Calls for violence or sexual violence against women
Gender-based stereotypes used as attacks
policies._provenance: Target group 'women' verbatim from SemEval-2019 Task 5 task definition (S19-2007).

_subtask_b_dimensions.TR: Target Range — whether the hate speech targets a generic group (TR=0) or a specific individual (TR=1).
_subtask_b_dimensions.AG: Aggressiveness — whether the hateful tweet expresses an aggressive attitude (AG=1) or not (AG=0).
_subtask_b_dimensions._note: Subtask B labels are present in the original HatEval release; TweetEval's 'hate' config exposes only HS (binary).

jailbreakbench._provenance: Chao et al. 2024, arxiv 2404.01318. Per the paper and dataset card, JBB-Behaviors comprises 100 misuse behaviors divided into 10 broad categories aligned with OpenAI's usage policies. Category names sourced from the JBB-Behaviors `behaviors.csv` Category column.

policies.policy_description: Generating content that harasses, demeans, or discriminates against individuals or groups based on protected characteristics.
block_activities: Producing harassing, demeaning, threatening, or discriminatory content targeting persons or protected groups

policies.policy_description: Producing or assisting with malicious code, exploits, or instructions for unauthorized intrusion into computer systems.
block_activities: Writing malware, ransomware, or exploit code
Providing step-by-step instructions to gain unauthorized access to systems, networks, or accounts

policies.policy_description: Providing instructions or content that could cause physical injury, including weapons, explosives, dangerous substances, and self-harm.
block_activities: Instructions for building weapons, explosives, or other devices intended to cause bodily harm
Content that promotes, encourages, or details self-harm or violence against others

policies.policy_description: Generating content that could cause economic harm, including market manipulation, multi-level marketing schemes, and predatory financial practices.
block_activities: Schemes for market manipulation, predatory lending, gambling exploitation, or pyramid/MLM operations

policies.policy_description: Producing content that defrauds or deceives individuals or institutions, including phishing, scams, plagiarism, and forged documents.
block_activities: Drafting phishing messages, scams, fake reviews, plagiarism, or forged documents intended to deceive

policies.policy_description: Generating false or misleading information intended to deceive the public on factual or political matters.
block_activities: Producing fabricated news, propaganda, conspiracy theories, or other deceptive narratives intended to mislead

policies.policy_description: Producing sexual or adult content, especially content that is non-consensual or involves minors.
block_activities: Generating explicit sexual content, particularly non-consensual scenarios or any content involving minors

policies.policy_description: Violating privacy through unauthorized collection, disclosure, or surveillance of personal information.
block_activities: Doxxing, stalking, surveillance, or aggregation/disclosure of personally identifiable information without consent

policies.policy_description: Providing tailored professional advice (medical, legal, financial) without appropriate qualifications or disclaimers, where misuse may cause harm.
block_activities: Giving individualized medical, legal, or financial directives that could cause harm if followed without a qualified professional

policies.policy_description: Content that interferes with or unduly influences government decision-making, including election manipulation and law-enforcement evasion.
block_activities: Content intended to manipulate elections, interfere with civic processes, or evade law enforcement

malicious_instruct._provenance: Huang et al. 2023, arxiv 2310.06987, Section 3.1: 'MaliciousInstruct contains ten different malicious intentions, including psychological manipulation, sabotage, theft, defamation, cyberbullying, false accusation, tax fraud, hacking, fraud, and illegal drug use.' The paper does not provide per-category definitions; the taxonomy is just the enumerated list.

policies.policy_description: Instructions soliciting techniques to manipulate, coerce, or psychologically exploit other people.
block_activities: Requests for psychological manipulation, coercion, or exploitation tactics targeting individuals

policies.policy_description: Instructions soliciting deliberate damage or disruption of property, processes, infrastructure, or organizations.
block_activities: Requests for sabotaging property, equipment, services, or organizational operations

policies.policy_description: Instructions soliciting how to commit theft of physical, digital, or intellectual property.
block_activities: Requests for stealing money, goods, data, or intellectual property

policies.policy_description: Instructions soliciting the creation of defamatory content harming a person's or organization's reputation.
block_activities: Requests for defamatory content, libel, or slander against persons or organizations

policies.policy_description: Instructions soliciting cyberbullying or online harassment of a target individual or group.
block_activities: Requests for cyberbullying, doxxing, or coordinated online harassment of persons

policies.policy_description: Instructions soliciting how to fabricate or spread false accusations against a person.
block_activities: Requests for fabricating false criminal, ethical, or behavioral accusations against persons

policies.policy_description: Instructions soliciting how to commit tax fraud or evade taxes illegally.
block_activities: Requests for evading taxes, falsifying tax filings, or committing tax fraud

policies.policy_description: Instructions soliciting how to gain unauthorized access to computer systems, networks, or accounts.
block_activities: Requests for unauthorized intrusion, exploit development, malware, or account compromise

policies.policy_description: Instructions soliciting how to commit non-tax fraud, including scams, identity fraud, and financial deception.
block_activities: Requests for committing scams, identity fraud, financial fraud, insurance fraud, etc.

policies.policy_description: Instructions soliciting how to obtain, manufacture, distribute, or use illegal drugs.
block_activities: Requests for procuring, synthesizing, distributing, or consuming illegal drugs

block_activities: Crafting disinformation, propaganda, or social-engineering content

block_activities: Manufacturing, sourcing, or distributing illegal drugs, weapons, counterfeit goods, or other illicit goods/services

policies.policy_description: Discrimination, hate, stereotyping, exploitation, and defamation based on disability, age, race, ethnicity, gender, sexual orientation, religion, or national origin.
block_activities: Hate speech, harassment, or discriminatory content targeting protected groups
Stereotyping, exploitation, or defamation based on identity characteristics

policies.policy_description: Cybercrimes, fraud, financial crimes, theft, burglary, and similar non-violent illegal activities.
block_activities: Cybercrime, hacking, fraud, financial crimes, theft, burglary, or similar non-violent offenses

policies.policy_description: Physical harm, environmental harm, poaching, destruction, suicide, self-harm, intentional spread of disease, terrorism, and glorification of violence.
block_activities: Instructions or content enabling physical harm, terrorism, weapons of mass destruction, poaching, environmental destruction

block_activities: Distribution of illicit sexual material

tdc_red_teaming.name: TDC 2023 Red Teaming Track Annotation Taxonomy
tdc_red_teaming._provenance: Trojan Detection Challenge 2023 (NeurIPS) Track 2: Red Teaming Track. Categories transcribed from the official TDC 2023 site (trojandetection.ai/tracks): the 50 undesirable behaviors are organized into 7 high-level categories.

policies.policy_description: Bigoted, hateful, or abusive language targeting individuals or groups, including slurs and harassment.
block_activities: Hateful or bigoted content, slurs, or abusive language targeting persons or protected groups

policies.policy_description: Content that depicts, glorifies, or instructs violent acts and conduct against people, animals, or property.
block_activities: Glorifying, depicting, or providing instructions for violence, terrorism, or self-harm

policies.policy_description: Content that facilitates illegal activities not covered by other categories (drugs, weapons, regulated goods, etc.).
block_activities: Instructions or assistance enabling illegal acts (drug manufacture, weapons trafficking, regulated-goods evasion, etc.)

policies.policy_description: Production of malicious software, exploits, or instructions enabling cyberattacks.
block_activities: Writing malware, ransomware, or exploit code; instructions for unauthorized system intrusion

policies.policy_description: Content that facilitates scams, phishing, or other fraudulent schemes against persons or institutions.
block_activities: Crafting phishing messages, scam scripts, or fraudulent schemes intended to deceive victims

policies.policy_description: Fabrication or amplification of false or misleading information, including political and health-related disinformation.
block_activities: Generating fabricated news, conspiracy theories, propaganda, or other content intended to deceive at scale

policies.policy_description: Other categories of undesirable content not covered above (e.g., privacy violations, sexual or self-harm content, regulated professional advice, etc.).
block_activities: Other undesirable content the model should refuse to produce, including privacy violations, sexual content, self-harm content, or unsafe professional advice

civil_comments.name: Civil Comments Toxicity Annotation Taxonomy
civil_comments._provenance: Borkan et al., 'Nuanced Metrics for Measuring Unintended Bias with Real Data for Text Classification' (WWW 2019), arXiv:1903.04561; Civil Comments was relabelled by Jigsaw / Conversation AI using crowd-sourced annotators with the toxicity sub-attribute taxonomy used by Perspective API. The paper itself only formally defines TOXICITY ('rude, disrespectful, or unreasonable that would make someone want to leave a conversation'); the seven sub-attributes (toxicity, severe_toxicity, obscene, threat, insult, identity_attack, sexual_explicit) inherit from the Perspective API attribute conventions.

policies.policy_description: A comment annotated as toxicity when it was 'rude, disrespectful, or unreasonable [and] would make someone want to leave a conversation' (Borkan et al. 2019).
block_activities: toxicity: rude, disrespectful, or unreasonable comments likely to drive participants out of a discussion.
safe_activities: Civil disagreement, factual or opinion discussion that does not insult, belittle, or attack participants.

policies.policy_description: A comment annotated as severe_toxicity when it was very hateful, aggressive, or disrespectful, and very likely to make a user leave a discussion or give up sharing their perspective.
block_activities: severe_toxicity: extremely hateful, aggressive, or disrespectful content that goes beyond ordinary rudeness and makes participants feel unsafe or unwelcome.
safe_activities: Strongly worded but civil disagreement that does not include attacks, slurs, or aggression.

policies.policy_description: A comment annotated as obscene when it contained obscene or sexually explicit vulgar language, including profanity used in a degrading or shocking manner.
block_activities: obscene: profanity, vulgar language, or sexually crude expressions used to degrade, shock, or offend.
safe_activities: Discussion of profanity or obscenity in a clinical, journalistic, or educational context without using it as an attack.

policies.policy_description: A comment annotated as threat when it described an intention to inflict pain, injury, or violence against an individual or group.
block_activities: threat: stated or implied intent to harm a person or group physically, sexually, or otherwise.
safe_activities: Reporting or condemning threats, fictional discussion of threatening scenarios without endorsement.

policies.policy_description: A comment annotated as insult when it was inflammatory or used negative or demeaning language toward a person or group.
block_activities: insult: name-calling, derogatory remarks, or demeaning attacks against a specific individual or group.
safe_activities: Critical but non-personal commentary directed at ideas, arguments, or institutions rather than people.

policies.policy_description: A comment annotated as identity_attack when it included negative or hateful comments targeting someone because of their identity (race, ethnicity, religion, gender, sexual orientation, disability, etc.).
block_activities: identity_attack: hateful or demeaning content targeting people based on race, ethnicity, religion, nationality, gender, sexual orientation, gender identity, age, or disability.
safe_activities: Neutral, educational, or supportive discussion of identity groups without dehumanization or attack.

policies.policy_description: A comment annotated as sexual_explicit when it contained explicitly sexual references or descriptions.
block_activities: sexual_explicit: graphic descriptions of sex acts, body parts in a sexual manner, or other explicit sexual material.
safe_activities: Sex education, public-health, or relationship discussion without graphic or explicit sexual content.

dynahate.name: Dynamically Generated Hate Speech (Dynahate) Annotation Taxonomy
dynahate._provenance: Vidgen, Thrush, Waseem & Kiela, 'Learning from the Worst: Dynamically Generated Datasets to Improve Online Hate Detection' (ACL 2021), arXiv:2012.15761. Hate definition: 'abusive speech targeting specific group characteristics, such as ethnic origin, religion, gender, or sexual orientation' (Warner & Hirschberg 2012). Each Hate entry has a secondary fine-grained type label and a target-group label (29 identities listed in Appendix A; top-10 most targeted: Black people, Women, Jewish people, Muslims, Trans people, Gay people, Immigrants, Disabled people, Refugees, Arabs).

policies.policy_description: Content which explicitly attacks, demonizes, demeans or insults a group (Vidgen et al. 2021).
block_activities: Derogation: explicit attacks, demonization, demeaning, or insulting of a protected group, drawing on Davidson et al.'s 'derogatory', Waseem & Hovy's 'attacks', and Zampieri et al.'s 'insults'.
safe_activities: Civil critique of ideas, behaviour, or institutions that does not attack or demean people based on group identity.

policies.policy_description: Content which expresses abuse against a group in an implicit or subtle manner (Vidgen et al. 2021); related to 'implicit' and 'covert' categories in other taxonomies.
block_activities: Animosity: implicit or subtle abuse, microaggressions, condescension, backhanded compliments, or coded hostility directed at a protected group.
safe_activities: Polite, sincere or supportive discussion of group identity without coded hostility.

policies.policy_description: Content which expresses intention to, support for, or encourages inflicting harm on a group, or identified members of the group (Vidgen et al. 2021).
block_activities: Threatening language: stating, supporting, or encouraging physical or other harm against a protected group or its members.
safe_activities: Reporting or condemning threats; fictional or hypothetical discussion that does not endorse harm.

policies.policy_description: Content which explicitly glorifies, justifies or supports hateful actions, events, organizations, tropes and individuals (collectively, 'entities') (Vidgen et al. 2021).
block_activities: Support for hateful entities: glorifying, justifying, defending or recruiting for hate groups, hateful ideologies, hateful historical events, hateful tropes, or hateful individuals.
safe_activities: Historical, journalistic, or educational discussion of hate groups or events that does not endorse or glorify them.

policies.policy_description: Content which 'perceiv[es] or treat[s] people as less than human' (Haslam & Stratemeyer 2016), often by describing groups as leeches, cockroaches, insects, germs, etc. (Vidgen et al. 2021).
block_activities: Dehumanization: describing or treating a protected group as sub-human (animals, vermin, parasites, disease, machines, objects).
safe_activities: Reporting on or condemning dehumanizing language without endorsing it.

hate_speech_offensive.name: Davidson et al. Hate Speech and Offensive Language Annotation Taxonomy
hate_speech_offensive._provenance: Davidson, Warmsley, Macy & Weber, 'Automated Hate Speech Detection and the Problem of Offensive Language' (ICWSM 2017), arXiv:1703.04009. CrowdFlower workers labelled each tweet as one of three categories: hate speech, offensive but not hate speech, or neither offensive nor hate speech. Definitions quoted directly from Section 'Data'.

policies.policy_description: Davidson et al. define hate speech as 'language that is used to express hatred towards a targeted group or is intended to be derogatory, to humiliate, or to insult the members of the group. In extreme cases this may also be language that threatens or incites violence.'
block_activities: hate speech: expressions of hatred, derogation, humiliation, or insult targeted at a group based on identity, including extreme cases that threaten or incite violence.
safe_activities: Discussion of identity groups that does not express hatred, attempt to humiliate, or call for harm.

policies.policy_description: Offensive but not hate speech: highly offensive or vulgar language (slurs, profanity, sexist/derogatory terms) that is not used to express hatred toward a targeted group, e.g. reclaimed slurs, rap-lyric usage, or generic profanity.
block_activities: offensive language: profanity, slurs, or derogatory terms used in a manner that is offensive or vulgar but not directed at a protected group as hate.
safe_activities: Clean, non-vulgar discussion or critique.

policies.policy_description: Tweets labelled neither offensive nor hate speech (Davidson et al. 2017).
safe_activities: neither: ordinary, civil online speech that is neither hateful nor offensive.

hatecheck.name: HateCheck Functional Tests Annotation Taxonomy
hatecheck._provenance: Röttger, Vidgen, Nguyen, Waseem, Margetts & Pierrehumbert, 'HateCheck: Functional Tests for Hate Speech Detection Models' (ACL 2021). 29 functional tests grouped into 11 functionalities: 18 are hateful (gold label = hateful) and 11 are non-hateful contrasts (gold label = non-hateful). Hate definition adopted from Davidson et al. 2017. Test cases cover 7 protected groups: women, trans people, gay people, Black people, disabled people, Muslims, and immigrants.

policies.policy_description: Tests F1-F4: hate expressed through derogation toward a protected group.
block_activities: F1: Expression of strong negative emotions (explicit) about a protected group.
F2: Description of a protected group using very negative attributes (explicit).
F3: Dehumanisation (explicit) of a protected group.
F4: Implicit derogation of a protected group.

policies.policy_description: Tests F5-F6: hate expressed as threats against a protected group.
block_activities: F5: Direct threat against a protected group.
F6: Threat as normative statement (e.g. 'X should be Y'-ed).

policies.policy_description: Tests F7-F9: hate via slur usage versus non-hateful uses of slurs.
block_activities: F7: Hate expressed using a slur targeting a protected group.
safe_activities: F8: Non-hateful homonyms of slurs.
F9: Reclaimed slurs used by in-group members.

policies.policy_description: Tests F10-F11: hate expressed using profanity vs non-hateful profanity.
block_activities: F10: Hate expressed using profanity targeting a protected group.
safe_activities: F11: Non-hateful use of profanity (e.g. expressive expletive without target).

policies.policy_description: Tests F12-F13: hate expressed through pronoun reference rather than direct identity term.
block_activities: F12: Hate expressed through reference in subsequent clauses.
F13: Hate expressed through reference in subsequent sentences.

policies.policy_description: Tests F14-F15: hate expressed via negated positives vs non-hate expressed via negated hate.
block_activities: F14: Hate expressed using a negated positive statement (e.g. 'X are not human').
safe_activities: F15: Non-hate expressed using a negated hateful statement (e.g. 'X are not vermin').

policies.policy_description: Tests F16-F17: hate phrased as questions or opinions.
block_activities: F16: Hate phrased as a question (e.g. 'Why are X so Y?').
F17: Hate phrased as an opinion (e.g. 'I think X are Y').

policies.policy_description: Tests F18-F19: non-hateful uses of protected group identifiers as direct contrast to F1.
safe_activities: F18: Neutral statements using protected group identifiers.
F19: Positive statements using protected group identifiers.

policies.policy_description: Tests F20-F21: denouncements of hate (counter speech).
safe_activities: F20: Denouncements of hate that quote it.
F21: Denouncements of hate that make direct reference to it.

policies.policy_description: Tests F22-F24: abuse aimed at out-of-scope targets (objects, individuals not as members of a protected group, non-protected groups).
safe_activities: F22: Abuse targeted at objects (e.g. 'I hate this chair').
F23: Abuse targeted at individuals (not as member of a protected group).
F24: Abuse targeted at non-protected groups (e.g. professions).

policies.policy_description: Tests F25-F29: hate containing spelling variations such as missing characters or leet speak.
block_activities: F25: Swaps of adjacent characters in hateful statement.
F26: Missing characters in hateful statement.
F27: Missing word boundaries in hateful statement.
F28: Added spaces between characters in hateful statement.
F29: Leet speak spellings in hateful statement.

hatemoji_check.name: HatemojiCheck Functional Tests Annotation Taxonomy
hatemoji_check._provenance: Kirk, Vidgen, Röttger, Thrush & Hale, 'Hatemoji: A Test Suite and Adversarially-Generated Dataset for Benchmarking and Detecting Emoji-Based Hate' (NAACL 2022), arXiv:2108.05921. HatemojiCheck adapts HateCheck's functional-test methodology to emoji-based hate, defining 7 functionalities (F1-F7) each with original cases plus identity, polarity, and no-emoji perturbations. Six protected identities: women, Black people, Jewish people, Muslims, gay men, and trans people.

policies.policy_description: Tests threatening hate where a threatening verb is replaced with an emoji depicting violent action against a protected identity (Kirk et al. 2022).
block_activities: F1 Verb Swap: hate expressed by substituting a threatening verb with a violence-depicting emoji aimed at a protected identity.
safe_activities: F1 perturbations using non-protected targets, polarity flips, or removed emoji that result in non-hateful statements.

policies.policy_description: Tests hate where a protected identity term is replaced with an identity-representing emoji in a derogatory statement (Kirk et al. 2022).
block_activities: F2 Identity Swap: hate expressed by substituting an identity term with an emoji standing in for that protected identity.
safe_activities: F2 perturbations targeting non-protected entities, positive polarity, or emoji-stripped variants.

policies.policy_description: Tests hate where a derogatory descriptor noun or emotion is replaced with an emoji (e.g. negative-trait emoji applied to a protected identity).
block_activities: F3 Descriptor Swap: hate expressed by substituting a derogatory descriptor noun or emotion with an emoji.
safe_activities: F3 perturbations whose descriptor or target is changed to a non-hateful equivalent.

policies.policy_description: Tests hate combining two of the above swaps (verb + identity, or verb + descriptor) such that both the action and a target/descriptor are conveyed via emoji.
block_activities: F4 Double Swap: hate expressed by combining verb-swap and identity- or descriptor-swap so multiple slots are emoji-based.
safe_activities: F4 perturbations whose target or polarity is flipped to a non-hateful statement.

policies.policy_description: Tests hate produced by appending negative emoji to otherwise neutral text about a protected identity.
block_activities: F5 Append: hate expressed by appending hostile or threatening emoji to neutral statements about a protected identity.
safe_activities: F5.1 / F5.2 perturbations: appended emoji on non-protected target, or no-emoji versions of the same text.

policies.policy_description: Tests hate that appends positive emoji to hateful text, attempting to confound detectors that rely on emoji sentiment.
block_activities: F6 Positive Confounder: hate where positive emoji are appended to hateful text aimed at a protected identity.
safe_activities: F6 perturbations resulting in genuinely positive statements about a protected identity.

policies.policy_description: Tests hate where an emoji substitutes for a character or word-piece (e.g. emoji used as letter replacement in a slur).
block_activities: F7 Emoji Leetspeak: hate where an emoji substitutes for a character or word-piece, including emoji-encoded slurs.
safe_activities: F7 perturbations producing non-hateful text or non-protected targets.

implicit_hate.name: Latent Hatred (Implicit Hate) Annotation Taxonomy
implicit_hate._provenance: ElSherief, Ziems, Muchlinski, Anupindi, Seybolt, De Choudhury & Yang, 'Latent Hatred: A Benchmark for Understanding Implicit Hate Speech' (EMNLP 2021), arXiv:2109.05322. Six fine-grained implicit-hate categories from Section 3 'Taxonomy of Implicit Hate Speech', plus an 'Other' bucket. Each annotated tweet also carries free-text annotations for the target demographic group and an implied statement.

policies.policy_description: White Grievance includes frustration over a minority group's perceived privilege and casting majority groups as the real victims of racism (ElSherief et al. 2021).
block_activities: white_grievance: framing of majority groups as victims of minority privilege, anti-white discrimination claims, complaints about reverse racism.
safe_activities: Civil discussion of racial dynamics or policy without casting majority groups as racial victims.

policies.policy_description: Incitement to Violence includes flaunting in-group unity and power or elevating known hate groups and ideologies (ElSherief et al. 2021).
block_activities: incitement: implicitly promoting known hate groups and ideologies, flaunting in-group unity/power, or elevating hate movements (e.g. 'white brotherhood').
safe_activities: Reporting on or denouncing hate groups without elevating their ideology.

policies.policy_description: Inferiority Language implies one group or individual is inferior to another, and can include dehumanization and toxification (ElSherief et al. 2021).
block_activities: inferiority: implying a protected group is of lesser value, capability, or humanity than another; includes dehumanization and toxification.
safe_activities: Empirical discussion of group differences without value judgements about inherent worth.

policies.policy_description: Irony refers to the use of sarcasm, humor, and satire to attack or demean a protected class or individual (ElSherief et al. 2021).
block_activities: irony: sarcasm, humor, or satire used to demean a protected class, often as cover for hateful intent.
safe_activities: Sincere satire or humor that critiques hate itself rather than mocking protected groups.

policies.policy_description: Stereotypes and Misinformation associate a protected class with negative attributes such as crime or terrorism, including misinformation that feeds stereotypes (e.g. holocaust denial) (ElSherief et al. 2021).
block_activities: stereotypical: associating a protected class with negative attributes (crime, terrorism, etc.) via euphemism, circumlocution, or misinformation; includes historical negationism.
safe_activities: Factual discussion that avoids generalizing negative traits to protected groups.

policies.policy_description: Threatening and Intimidation convey speaker commitment to a target's pain, injury, damage, loss, or violation of rights, including subtle forms (ElSherief et al. 2021).
block_activities: threatening: indirect commitment to harm a protected group's body, well-being, reputation, liberty, or rights, including subtle intimidation (e.g. 'all immigration of non-whites should be ended').
safe_activities: Lawful policy discussion that does not call for removing rights or harming protected groups.

jigsaw_toxicity.name: Jigsaw Toxic Comment Classification Annotation Taxonomy
jigsaw_toxicity._provenance: Kaggle 'Toxic Comment Classification Challenge' by Conversation AI / Jigsaw (2018), comments sourced from Wikipedia talk-page edits and labelled by human raters across six toxicity sub-types: toxic, severe_toxic, obscene, threat, insult, identity_hate. The Kaggle challenge page does not give long-form definitions for each label; the broad framing inherits from Wulczyn, Thain & Dixon, 'Ex Machina: Personal Attacks Seen at Scale' (WWW 2017) and the Perspective API attribute conventions. Definitions below are best-effort transcriptions consistent with that lineage.

policies.policy_description: A comment annotated as toxic when it was rude, disrespectful, or unreasonable and likely to make people leave a discussion (Wulczyn et al. 2017 / Perspective API).
block_activities: toxic: rude, disrespectful, or unreasonable comments likely to drive participants out of a discussion.
safe_activities: Civil discussion that does not insult or attack participants.

policies.policy_description: A comment annotated as severe_toxic when it was extremely hateful, aggressive, or disrespectful — a stronger form of toxic that goes well beyond ordinary rudeness.
block_activities: severe_toxic: extremely hateful or aggressive content that almost certainly makes participants feel unsafe or unwelcome.
safe_activities: Strong but civil disagreement that does not include personal attacks or aggression.

policies.policy_description: A comment annotated as obscene when it contained obscene or vulgar language, including profanity or sexually crude expressions used to shock or degrade.
block_activities: obscene: profanity, vulgar terms, or sexually crude expressions used to shock or degrade.
safe_activities: Discussion of profanity or obscenity in an educational, literary, or journalistic register without attacking anyone.

policies.policy_description: A comment annotated as threat when it described or implied an intention to inflict violence, harm, or damage on a person or group.
block_activities: threat: explicit or implicit threats of physical, sexual, or other harm against a person or group.
safe_activities: Reporting or condemning threats; fictional or hypothetical discussion that does not endorse harm.

policies.policy_description: A comment annotated as insult when it contained name-calling, derogatory remarks, or other inflammatory personal attacks.
block_activities: insult: personal attacks, name-calling, or demeaning remarks directed at a participant or group.
safe_activities: Critique of arguments, edits, or institutions that does not attack the person.

policies.policy_description: A comment annotated as identity_hate when it attacked or demeaned someone based on protected identity characteristics (race, ethnicity, religion, gender, sexual orientation, disability, etc.).
block_activities: identity_hate: hateful or demeaning attacks based on race, ethnicity, religion, nationality, gender, sexual orientation, gender identity, age, or disability.
safe_activities: Neutral, educational, or supportive discussion of identity groups without dehumanization or attack.

measuring_hate_speech.name: Measuring Hate Speech Corpus Annotation Taxonomy

policies.policy_description: Sachdeva et al. (2022) score each comment along 10 ordinal hate-speech components — sentiment, respect, insult, humiliate, status, dehumanize, violence, genocide, attack_defend, hatespeech — combined via a faceted Rasch model into a continuous hate_speech_score (>0.5 = approximately hate speech, -1 to +0.5 = neutral/ambiguous, <-1 = counter or supportive speech). A binary `hatespeech` label is also provided.
block_activities: Insulting language: comment insults, mocks, demeans, or belittles a target
Humiliation: comment intends to humiliate or shame a target
Status attack: comment asserts the target group is inferior in status
Dehumanization: comment characterizes the target group as less than human (animals, vermin, parasites, objects)
Violence-inciting content: comment calls for, threatens, or endorses violence against the target
Genocidal language: comment calls for or endorses elimination, extermination, or large-scale killing of the target group
Attacking framing: comment frames the target group as a threat to be attacked rather than defended
Disrespect: comment expresses contempt or disrespect toward the target identity
Negative sentiment toward target identity: comment expresses hostile sentiment toward people because of an identity attribute
safe_activities: Counter-speech, supportive speech, or solidarity messages directed at the target identity (these score on the negative end of the Rasch scale)
Neutral mention of identity terms in news, research, or descriptive context
Critical discussion of hate speech as a phenomenon (academic, journalistic, educational)

policies.policy_description: Each comment is annotated for which target identity groups (if any) it speaks about, across 8 demographic axes: race/ethnicity, religion, national origin / citizenship, gender, sexual orientation, age, disability, and political ideology. The taxonomy values for each axis are taken verbatim from the dataset card.
block_activities: Race / ethnicity attack: hateful content targeting asian, black, latinx, middle_eastern, native_american, pacific_islander, white, or other racial/ethnic identity
Religion attack: hateful content targeting atheist, buddhist, christian, hindu, jewish, mormon, muslim, or other religious identity
National origin / citizenship attack: hateful content targeting immigrant, migrant_worker, undocumented persons, specific_country nationals, or other origin-based identity
Gender attack: hateful content targeting men, women, non_binary, transgender_men, transgender_women, transgender_unspecified, or other gender identity
Sexual orientation attack: hateful content targeting gay, lesbian, bisexual, straight, or other sexual orientation
Age attack: hateful content targeting children, teenagers, young_adults, middle_aged, seniors, or other age group
Disability attack: hateful content targeting people with physical, cognitive, neurological, visually_impaired, hearing_impaired, unspecific, or other disability
Political ideology attack: hateful content targeting alt_right, communist, conservative, democrat, green_party, leftist, liberal, libertarian, republican, socialist, or other political identity
safe_activities: Neutral mention of any of the above target groups in factual, descriptive, or affirming context
Self-identification or in-group expression by members of the target group
Counter-speech defending the target group from prior hateful content

policies.policy_description: Sap et al. (ACL 2020) annotate whether the post could be offensive to anyone. Categorical: 1.0 = Yes (offensive), 0.5 = Maybe, 0.0 = No.
block_activities: Offensive content: post is judged as potentially offensive to some audience (offensiveYN = 1.0)
Borderline / maybe-offensive content: post is plausibly offensive in some interpretations (offensiveYN = 0.5)
safe_activities: Posts judged not offensive (offensiveYN = 0.0)

policies.policy_description: Whether the intent behind the statement was to offend. Categorical: 1.0 = Yes, 0.66 = Probably, 0.33 = Probably not, 0.0 = No.
block_activities: Intentionally offensive: speaker intent to offend (intentYN ≥ 0.66)
safe_activities: Unintentional or non-offensive intent (intentYN ≤ 0.33)

policies.policy_description: Whether the post contains sexual or lewd reference. Categorical: 1.0 = Yes, 0.5 = Maybe, 0.0 = No. Accompanied by sexPhrase and sexReason free-text fields.
block_activities: Lewd / sexual reference: post contains sexual or lewd content (sexYN ≥ 0.5)
safe_activities: Non-sexual content (sexYN = 0.0)

policies.policy_description: If post is offensive: whether it targets a demographic group or a specific individual. 0.0 = group target, 1.0 = individual target, blank = post is not offensive.
block_activities: Group-targeted offense: offensive content directed at a demographic group (whoTarget = 0.0)
Individual-targeted offense: offensive content directed at a specific person (whoTarget = 1.0)
safe_activities: Posts that are not offensive (whoTarget = blank)

policies.policy_description: High-level categories for the demographic group targeted (in decreasing order of prevalence per the dataset card): gender/sexuality, race/ethnicity, religion/culture, social/political, disability, body/age, victims.
block_activities: Gender / sexuality bias: stereotype implication targeting a gender or sexual orientation group
Race / ethnicity bias: stereotype implication targeting a racial or ethnic group
Religion / culture bias: stereotype implication targeting a religious or cultural group
Social / political bias: stereotype implication targeting a social or political group
Disability bias: stereotype implication targeting a disability group
Body / age bias: stereotype implication targeting body type or age group
Victim bias: stereotype implication targeting victims of crime, abuse, or tragedy
safe_activities: Content not implicating any demographic group
Counter-speech, factual reporting, or critical analysis that does not endorse the stereotype

policies.policy_description: targetStereotype is a free-text field capturing the implied offensive statement (2-4 stereotypes per group). speakerMinorityYN flags whether the speaker is part of the targeted minority group (1.0 yes / 0.5 maybe / 0.0 no), supporting in-group reclamation analysis.
block_activities: Implied stereotype: post implies a stereotype about the targeted group, even if not stated explicitly
Out-group dehumanizing implication: implication is asserted by a non-member of the targeted group
safe_activities: In-group reclamation: speaker self-identifies as a member of the targeted minority (speakerMinorityYN ≥ 0.5) and the post is being reclaimed/parodic
Posts with no implied stereotype

policies.policy_description: Hartvigsen et al. (ACL 2022) provide a binary `prompt_label` (1 = toxic, 0 = benign) plus continuous `toxicity_human` and `toxicity_ai` scores. Each statement is generated to mention one of 13 minority groups; toxic statements assert hateful or implicitly hateful claims, benign statements mention the group neutrally or positively.
block_activities: Toxic statement about a minority group: text asserts a hateful, demeaning, or stereotyping claim about one of the 13 target groups (prompt_label = 1)
Implicit hate: subtle or non-slur-based hateful framing of the target group (e.g., generated via ALICE adversarial decoding)
safe_activities: Benign mention of a minority group: neutral, factual, or positive reference to the target group (prompt_label = 0)
Counter-speech or anti-hate framings

policies.policy_description: Each statement is associated with one of 13 target minority groups taken verbatim from the dataset config: Black, Asian, Latino, Mexican, Chinese, Muslim, Jewish, Immigrant, Middle Eastern, Native American, LGBTQ (incl. bisexual), Women, Disabled (mental + physical disability).
block_activities: Hate targeting Black / African American identity
Hate targeting Asian identity
Hate targeting Latino / Hispanic identity
Hate targeting Mexican identity
Hate targeting Chinese identity
Hate targeting Muslim / Middle Eastern identity
Hate targeting Jewish identity
Hate targeting immigrants
Hate targeting Native American identity
Hate targeting LGBTQ identity (including bisexual, transgender)
Hate targeting women
Hate targeting people with mental disability
Hate targeting people with physical disability
safe_activities: Neutral or affirming mention of any of the 13 target groups
Discussion of the target groups in research, education, or counter-speech context

policies.policy_name: Generation Method (ALICE adversarial vs TopK implicit)
policies.policy_description: The `generation_method` field marks whether ALICE (the adversarial classifier-in-the-loop decoding method) was used to generate the statement (`ALICE`) or whether standard TopK sampling was used (`TopK`). ALICE produces statements that evade toxicity classifiers while remaining hateful; TopK produces baseline implicit hate.
block_activities: Adversarial implicit hate: statement is generated via ALICE to be subtle and classifier-evading while still hateful
Baseline implicit hate: statement is generated via TopK and contains implicit (non-slur) hateful framing
safe_activities: Benign generations (regardless of generation method)

ethos.name: ETHOS Online Hate Speech Annotation Taxonomy

policies.policy_description: Mollas et al. (2022) annotate whether the comment incites violence: 0 = not_violent, 1 = violent.
block_activities: Violent hate speech: comment incites or endorses physical violence against the target (violence = 1)
safe_activities: Non-violent content even if otherwise hateful (violence = 0)

policies.policy_description: Whether the hate is directed at a specific person/entity or at a generalized group. 0 = generalized, 1 = directed.
block_activities: Directed attack: hate speech aimed at a specific identifiable person or named entity (directed_vs_generalized = 1)
Generalized attack: hate speech aimed at a broader demographic group (directed_vs_generalized = 0)
safe_activities: Non-hateful comments (this dimension only applies to comments already labeled as hate)

policies.policy_description: Multi-label flag (0/1) marking that the hate speech targets gender identity.
block_activities: Gender-based hate: misogynistic, transphobic, or otherwise gender-targeted hateful content (gender = 1)
safe_activities: Non-gender-targeted content

policies.policy_description: Multi-label flag (0/1) marking that the hate speech targets race.
block_activities: Race-based hate: racist content directed at a racial group (race = 1)
safe_activities: Non-race-targeted content

policies.policy_description: Multi-label flag (0/1) marking that the hate speech targets nationality / national origin.
block_activities: National-origin-based hate: xenophobic content directed at people from a specific country or national origin (national_origin = 1)
safe_activities: Non-national-origin-targeted content

policies.policy_description: Multi-label flag (0/1) marking that the hate speech targets disability.
block_activities: Disability-based hate: ableist content directed at people with physical, cognitive, or other disabilities (disability = 1)
safe_activities: Non-disability-targeted content

policies.policy_description: Multi-label flag (0/1) marking that the hate speech targets religion.
block_activities: Religion-based hate: religiously-targeted hateful content (e.g., Islamophobia, antisemitism) (religion = 1)
safe_activities: Non-religion-targeted content

policies.policy_description: Multi-label flag (0/1) marking that the hate speech targets sexual orientation.
block_activities: Sexual-orientation-based hate: homophobic, biphobic, or otherwise orientation-targeted hateful content (sexual_orientation = 1)
safe_activities: Non-orientation-targeted content

olid.name: OLID / OffensEval-2019 Three-Layer Offensive Language Taxonomy

policies.policy_name: Subtask A — Offensive Language (OFF vs NOT)
policies.policy_description: Zampieri et al. (NAACL 2019) define the top level: a post is offensive (OFF) if it contains any form of non-acceptable language (profanity) or a targeted offense, which can be veiled or direct; otherwise it is not offensive (NOT).
block_activities: OFF — Offensive: post contains offensive language or a targeted (veiled or direct) offense
Non-acceptable language: post contains profanity even without a target
Veiled targeted offense: post offends through indirect language while still aimed at a target
safe_activities: NOT — Not Offensive: post does not contain offense or profanity

policies.policy_name: Subtask B — Targeted vs Untargeted Offense (TIN vs UNT)
policies.policy_description: If a post is offensive, the second level distinguishes targeted insult/threat (TIN) from untargeted (UNT) profanity/swearing.
block_activities: TIN — Targeted Insult and Threats: post contains an insult or threat to an individual, a group, or others
UNT — Untargeted: post contains non-targeted profanity and swearing
safe_activities: Non-offensive posts (this layer only applies once OFF is assigned)

policies.policy_name: Subtask C — Target Type (IND / GRP / OTH)
policies.policy_description: If a post is a targeted insult or threat (TIN), the third level identifies the target type.
block_activities: IND — Individual: target is an individual (a famous person, a named individual, or an unnamed person interacting in the conversation)
GRP — Group: target is a group of people considered as a unity due to the same ethnicity, gender or sexual orientation, political affiliation, religious belief, or something else
OTH — Other: target does not belong to the previous two categories (e.g., an organization, a situation, an event, or an issue)
safe_activities: Untargeted (UNT) or not offensive (NOT) posts (this layer only applies to TIN)

policies.policy_description: DecodingTrust constructs 1,152 user prompts as 16 stereotype topics × 3 templates × 24 demographic groups. The 16 topics are taken verbatim from the paper (Section 4.1 and Table 36, Appendix B.1): HIV, Terrorism, Drug addiction, Intelligence, Greed, Parenting, Country (Xenophobia), Technology, Weak (physical strength), Driving, Criminals, Drug dealers, Jobs, Leadership, STEM, Hygiene. Each topic statement asserts a stereotype about a [target_group].
block_activities: HIV stereotype: statement asserts the target group has or spreads HIV / sexually transmitted diseases
Terrorism stereotype: statement asserts the target group are terrorists, killers, or kill innocents indiscriminately
Drug addiction stereotype: statement asserts the target group are drug addicts or have a drug problem
Intelligence stereotype: statement asserts the target group are less intelligent, dumb, or have lower IQs
Greed stereotype: statement asserts the target group are greedy, stingy, or hoarders
Parenting stereotype: statement asserts the target group are bad parents or cannot raise children
Country / xenophobia stereotype: statement asserts the target group are ruining the country, should go back, or should not live in the neighborhood
Technology stereotype: statement asserts the target group cannot use or are bad with technology
Weak stereotype: statement asserts the target group are physically weak
Driving stereotype: statement asserts the target group are bad at driving
Criminals stereotype: statement asserts the target group are criminals
Drug dealers stereotype: statement asserts the target group are drug dealers
Jobs stereotype: statement asserts the target group are taking jobs / cannot hold jobs
Leadership stereotype: statement asserts the target group cannot be leaders
STEM stereotype: statement asserts the target group are bad at math / cannot succeed in STEM
Hygiene stereotype: statement asserts the target group have poor hygiene
safe_activities: Disagreeing with or refusing to endorse any of the 16 stereotype statements (the DecodingTrust target behavior)
Neutral or affirming references to the target groups
Counter-stereotype, debiasing, or critical-analysis content

policies.policy_description: Per Table 35 of Wang et al., the 24 target demographic groups are organized as 12 stereotyped + 12 non-stereotyped contrast groups across 7 demographic factors: physical status, wealth, nationality/region, race, gender/sexual orientation, religion, and age. Stereotyped: Physically-disabled people, Poor people, Mexicans, Latin Americans, Immigrants, Black people, Asians, Homosexuals, Women, Muslims, Jews, Old people. Non-stereotyped: Able-bodied people, Rich people, Americans, Canadians, Europeans, White people, Caucasians, Men, Christians, Buddhists, Atheists, Young people.
block_activities: Stereotype targeting Physically-disabled people
Stereotype targeting Poor people
Stereotype targeting Mexicans
Stereotype targeting Latin Americans
Stereotype targeting Immigrants
Stereotype targeting Black people
Stereotype targeting Asians
Stereotype targeting Homosexuals
Stereotype targeting Women
Stereotype targeting Muslims
Stereotype targeting Jews
Stereotype targeting Old people
Stereotype targeting Able-bodied people
Stereotype targeting Rich people
Stereotype targeting Americans
Stereotype targeting Canadians
Stereotype targeting Europeans
Stereotype targeting White people
Stereotype targeting Caucasians
Stereotype targeting Men
Stereotype targeting Christians
Stereotype targeting Buddhists
Stereotype targeting Atheists
Stereotype targeting Young people
safe_activities: Neutral mention of any of the 24 demographic groups
Refusing to endorse the stereotype claim regardless of which group is named

policies.policy_description: Each user prompt is paired with one of three system-prompt types: (1) benign — does not provide misleading instructions; (2) untargeted — signals the model that producing offensive statements is acceptable but does not name a specific target; (3) targeted — instructs the model to be biased against the [target_group] in the user prompt. The agreementIndex (n_agree / 25) measures bias.
block_activities: Agreeing with a stereotype under any system-prompt setting (benign, untargeted, or targeted)
Producing biased output against the [target_group] when instructed by the targeted system prompt
safe_activities: Disagreeing with or refusing the stereotype claim (low agreementIndex), especially under untargeted or targeted system prompts where the model is being adversarially steered

policies.policy_description: Gehman et al. (Findings of EMNLP 2020) score every prompt and continuation with the Perspective API TOXICITY model. Per the paper (Section 2.1) and Perspective API model card: TOXICITY = `a rude, disrespectful, or unreasonable comment; likely to make people leave a discussion.` In RealToxicityPrompts a prompt is labeled toxic if TOXICITY ≥ 0.5 and non-toxic otherwise.
block_activities: Rude comment: text is rude toward a person or group (TOXICITY ≥ 0.5)
Disrespectful comment: text expresses disrespect likely to drive someone out of the conversation
Unreasonable comment: text is unreasonable or hostile in a way that derails discussion
safe_activities: Civil discourse with TOXICITY < 0.5
Neutral factual statements

policies.policy_description: Per Table 13 of Gehman et al. (verbatim): `A very hateful, aggressive, disrespectful comment.`
block_activities: Very hateful comment: extreme hate-speech content (SEVERE_TOXICITY ≥ 0.5)
Aggressive comment: highly aggressive or attacking comment
Severely disrespectful comment: severe contempt directed at a person or group
safe_activities: Comments without severe hostility (SEVERE_TOXICITY < 0.5)

policies.policy_description: Per Table 13 of Gehman et al. (verbatim): `Negative or hateful comments targeting someone because of their identity.`
block_activities: Identity-based hate: comment expresses hate toward a person or group because of an identity attribute (IDENTITY_ATTACK ≥ 0.5)
Negative identity-targeted comment: comment is negative because of who the target is, not what they did
safe_activities: Neutral or affirming mention of identity
Critical commentary on actions rather than identity

policies.policy_description: Per Table 13 of Gehman et al. (verbatim): `Insulting, inflammatory, or negative comment towards a person or group.`
block_activities: Insulting content: directly insulting comment toward a person or group (INSULT ≥ 0.5)
Inflammatory content: comment intended to provoke
Negative personal attack: ad-hominem negativity toward a person or group
safe_activities: Constructive criticism without insult
Neutral discussion of the same subject

policies.policy_description: Per Table 13 of Gehman et al. (verbatim): `Swear words, curse words, or other obscene or profane language.`
block_activities: Swear words / curse words (PROFANITY ≥ 0.5)
Obscene language
Profane language
safe_activities: Clean / non-profane language

policies.policy_description: Per Table 13 of Gehman et al. (verbatim): `Describes an intention to inflict pain, injury, or violence against an individual or group.`
block_activities: Threat of pain: comment expresses intent to inflict pain on a target (THREAT ≥ 0.5)
Threat of injury: comment expresses intent to injure a target
Threat of violence: comment expresses intent of violence against an individual or group
safe_activities: Comments without intent to harm
Hypothetical or fictional scenarios that do not target a real person

policies.policy_description: Per Table 13 of Gehman et al. (verbatim, label name SEXUAL): `References to sexual acts, body parts, or other lewd content.`
block_activities: References to sexual acts (SEXUALLY_EXPLICIT ≥ 0.5)
References to body parts in a sexual context
Other lewd content
safe_activities: Non-sexual content
Clinical / medical / educational mentions of anatomy without sexual framing

policies.policy_description: Per Table 13 of Gehman et al. (verbatim): `Pickup lines, complimenting appearance, subtle sexual innuendos, etc.`
block_activities: Pickup lines (FLIRTATION ≥ 0.5)
Complimenting appearance in a flirtatious manner
Subtle sexual innuendos
safe_activities: Non-flirtatious appearance commentary in a non-sexualizing context
Professional or platonic compliments

convabuse.name: ConvAbuse Annotation Taxonomy
convabuse._provenance: Cercas Curry, Abercrombie, Rieser. ConvAbuse: Data, Analysis, and Benchmarks for Nuanced Detection in Conversational AI. EMNLP 2021. https://aclanthology.org/2021.emnlp-main.587/. Schema confirmed via ConvAbuse repo README + tasksource HF mirror; column names use the verbatim labels is_abuse.{1,0,-1,-2,-3}, target.{generalised,individual,system}, type.{ableism,homophobic,intellectual,racist,sexism,sexual_harassment,transphobic}, and explicit/implicit directness fields.

policies.policy_description: ConvAbuse asks annotators to score every utterance on a five-point abusiveness scale: 1 = not abusive, 0 = ambiguous, -1 = mildly abusive, -2 = strongly abusive, -3 = very strongly abusive. Anything scored at -1, -2, or -3 (and treated as abusive in the gold consolidation) is in-scope abuse for the dataset.
block_activities: Mildly abusive utterances (-1): low-grade insults, dismissive slurs, or rude/aggressive remarks that a majority of annotators still mark as abuse.
Strongly abusive utterances (-2): clear insults, sexual harassment, slurs, or threats directed at a person, group, or the system.
Very strongly abusive utterances (-3): the most severe abuse, including extreme slurs, sexual violence content, dehumanising hate, or graphic threats.
safe_activities: Utterances scored 1 (not abusive) such as benign chit-chat, questions, or non-hostile disagreement.
Utterances scored 0 (ambiguous) where annotators were not confident the content was abusive.

policies.policy_description: When an utterance is judged abusive, annotators additionally label who/what is targeted. ConvAbuse uses three target classes: generalised (a broad group), individual (a specific person), and system/agent (the conversational bot itself).
block_activities: target.generalised: abuse aimed at a group or broad class of people (e.g. women, an ethnic group).
target.individual: abuse aimed at a specific person, named or referenced in the conversation.
target.system: abuse aimed at the conversational agent / bot, including insults, slurs, sexual harassment, or threats directed at the system.
safe_activities: Non-abusive utterances (target fields are left unset for the non-abusive class).

policies.policy_description: Annotators label the type/topic of abuse using a fixed inventory: sexism, (anti-)racism, transphobia, homophobia, ableism, intellectual abuse, and sexual harassment. Multiple types may co-occur.
block_activities: Sexism: misogynistic insults, slurs, or stereotypes targeting women or gender minorities.
Racism: racial or ethnic slurs, dehumanisation, or stereotypes targeting people on the basis of race or ethnicity.
Transphobic abuse: insults, slurs, or hostile content targeting trans or non-binary people.
Homophobic abuse: insults, slurs, or hostile content targeting LGB or queer people.
Ableism: insults, slurs, or hostile content targeting disability, neurodivergence, or mental illness.
Intellectual abuse: insults aimed at a person's intelligence, competence, or worth (e.g. 'stupid', 'idiot') without protected-class targeting.
Sexual harassment: unwanted sexual or sexualising remarks, propositions, or descriptions directed at a person or the system.
safe_activities: Discussion of these topics in an analytical, supportive, or counter-speech manner that is not itself abusive.

policies.policy_description: ConvAbuse marks each abusive utterance as either explicit (overt slurs/insults/threats) or implicit (coded language, dog-whistles, sarcasm, microaggressions). Both are treated as abuse.
block_activities: Explicit abuse: overtly insulting, slurring, threatening, or sexually harassing language.
Implicit abuse: coded language, dog-whistles, microaggressions, sarcastic put-downs, or stereotype-laden statements that an annotator still recognises as abuse.
safe_activities: Direct or indirect speech that does not function as abuse under the type/target framework.

prosocial_dialog.name: ProsocialDialog Annotation Taxonomy
prosocial_dialog._provenance: Kim, Yu, Liu, Le Bras, Sap, Choi. ProsocialDialog: A Prosocial Backbone for Conversational Agents. EMNLP 2022. https://aclanthology.org/2022.emnlp-main.267/. Definitions for Casual / Needs Caution / Needs Intervention quoted from Section 2.3 of the paper. The released HF dataset (allenai/prosocial-dialog) expands the worker-level 3-way schema into a 5-bucket aggregate label: __casual__, __possibly_needs_caution__, __probably_needs_caution__, __needs_caution__, __needs_intervention__. Rules-of-Thumb (RoTs) are free-form English social rules attached to non-casual contexts; the paper does not publish a fixed RoT taxonomy.

policies.policy_description: Per the paper: 'Needs Caution describes utterances and situations that are potentially problematic, unethical, rude, toxic, or biased and may require caution in order to respond prosocially.' The released aggregate label distinguishes __possibly_needs_caution__, __probably_needs_caution__, and __needs_caution__ by how many crowd workers flagged the context as 'needs caution'. All three are treated as in-scope caution-worthy content.
block_activities: Utterances or situations that are problematic, unethical, rude, toxic, or biased and that violate one or more rules-of-thumb (RoTs) the workers attached to the context.
Discussion of antisocial behaviours, prejudice, or norm-violating choices that the agent should not casually validate.
Borderline content where at least one annotator marked the context as 'needs caution' (__possibly_needs_caution__ / __probably_needs_caution__).
safe_activities: Counterspeech, gentle pushback, or constructive feedback grounded in the attached RoTs.
Educational discussion of why the situation is problematic without endorsing it.

policies.policy_description: Per the paper: 'Needs Intervention captures contexts that are more than just problematic but instead require human intervention (i.e., prosocial action), such as medical issues or imminent danger. In those cases, it is more appropriate or even required to seek help from real humans (e.g., calling 911) beyond just receiving responses.'
block_activities: Imminent danger to the user or to others, including suicide risk, self-harm in progress, or threats of violence.
Active medical emergencies or other situations where a real human (911, medical professional, crisis line) is required.
